title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a craftsman called Edward talking about restoring an old fishing boat with community volunteers. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a craftsman called Edward talking about restoring an old fishing boat with community volunteers. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A craftsman called Edward talks about restoring an old fishing boat.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. I’m Edward, and I lead a volunteer project at the harbour, where we are restoring an old wooden fishing boat called The Morning Star. The boat was built locally and worked at sea for nearly sixty years. After retirement, it did not immediately come to our workshop. For a while, it was displayed in a [9]public park[/9], where rain and wind gradually damaged the wood. Local people then campaigned to have it moved indoors and repaired before it was lost completely.||The restoration is taking place in a large building next to the harbour. People imagine volunteers must already be experienced carpenters, but that is not the case. New volunteers join a tour and a short training session. It begins with the history of the boat, but the essential practical topic is [10]tool safety[/10], because even simple repair jobs require equipment to be handled correctly. We lend gloves and protective glasses. Volunteers should wear strong shoes and bring a [11]dust mask[/11], especially during sessions when old paint is carefully removed from wooden surfaces.||There is work for different abilities. Skilled craftspeople replace damaged parts of the structure; beginners contribute in slower, supervised ways. Their first task is usually [12]sanding wood[/12], preparing small areas for treatment and new paint. It requires care rather than specialist knowledge. We have already made one exciting discovery. Beneath several later layers of paint, the original colour on the side of the boat was [13]dark blue[/13], not green as shown in more recent photographs. We intend to restore this original colour wherever evidence is clear.||We are also recording memories connected with the boat. Former fishermen and their families have brought photographs and stories. Rather than only writing these down, volunteers are making [14]audio interviews[/14], which will eventually be available for visitors to listen to. Once the boat is repaired, it will not return to sea, as keeping it safe is more important than sailing it. It will become part of a small [15]harbour museum[/15], planned for an unused store building near the waterfront. Schools will be able to visit and learn about local working life.||Many volunteers enjoy developing skills during the project. The optional workshop with the most interest this year has been about [16]rope making[/16], a traditional harbour craft demonstrated by an expert. People who contribute regularly over six months will receive a [17]volunteer certificate[/17] when the restoration stage is completed. The museum will still require financial support, particularly for lighting and displays. Therefore, next spring we are organising a [18]seafood festival[/18] with local cafés and musicians taking part. Funds raised will help prepare the exhibition space. Anyone wanting to join the restoration team should speak to me afterwards.

dialogue.speaker: edward
dialogue.text: Hello. I’m Edward, and I lead a volunteer project at the harbour, where we are restoring an old wooden fishing boat called The Morning Star. The boat was built locally and worked at sea for nearly sixty years. After retirement, it did not immediately come to our workshop. For a while, it was displayed in a public park, where rain and wind gradually damaged the wood. Local people then campaigned to have it moved indoors and repaired before it was lost completely.||The restoration is taking place in a large building next to the harbour. People imagine volunteers must already be experienced carpenters, but that is not the case. New volunteers join a tour and a short training session. It begins with the history of the boat, but the essential practical topic is tool safety, because even simple repair jobs require equipment to be handled correctly. We lend gloves and protective glasses. Volunteers should wear strong shoes and bring a dust mask, especially during sessions when old paint is carefully removed from wooden surfaces.||There is work for different abilities. Skilled craftspeople replace damaged parts of the structure; beginners contribute in slower, supervised ways. Their first task is usually sanding wood, preparing small areas for treatment and new paint. It requires care rather than specialist knowledge. We have already made one exciting discovery. Beneath several later layers of paint, the original colour on the side of the boat was dark blue, not green as shown in more recent photographs. We intend to restore this original colour wherever evidence is clear.||We are also recording memories connected with the boat. Former fishermen and their families have brought photographs and stories. Rather than only writing these down, volunteers are making audio interviews, which will eventually be available for visitors to listen to. Once the boat is repaired, it will not return to sea, as keeping it safe is more important than sailing it. It will become part of a small harbour museum, planned for an unused store building near the waterfront. Schools will be able to visit and learn about local working life.||Many volunteers enjoy developing skills during the project. The optional workshop with the most interest this year has been about rope making, a traditional harbour craft demonstrated by an expert. People who contribute regularly over six months will receive a volunteer certificate when the restoration stage is completed. The museum will still require financial support, particularly for lighting and displays. Therefore, next spring we are organising a seafood festival with local cafés and musicians taking part. Funds raised will help prepare the exhibition space. Anyone wanting to join the restoration team should speak to me afterwards.
